english: yid-_oṅ (yid 'ong) my dearest! Pth.; ṅa-ráṅ (nga rang) I myself, esp. col. very frq.; *ṅa raṅ-ka* (nga rang ka) Ts., *ṅa tsog* (nga tshog) Ü, ṅa nyid (nga nyid), ṅa kò-na (nga kho na), ṅa bdag (nga bdag) (?), ṅa-bo (nga bo) (??) Cs. id.; ṅa raṅ-gi yaṅ (nga rang gi yang) moreover, what concerns my own affairs Mil. Distinct expressions for the pl. we are: ṅá-cag (nga cag) B. and C.; *ṅá-ža* (nga zha) W., *ṅá-ya* (nga ya) Bal.; in W. *ṅá-ža* (nga zha) seems to be used in an exclusive sense: I and my people, i.e. excluding you or the person or persons addressed, so that when Europeans use it in Ld. or Lh., in addressing their hearers, meaning to include themselves (all of us, we and you), they are generally misunderstood; *ṅa daṅ* (nga dang) 'he or those with me', is said to be used in a similar manner; *ṅa daṅ nyis* (nga dang nyis) both of us; ṅa-rnams (nga rnams) we Cs. Synonyms are: ṅed (nged), ṅos (ngos), bdag (bdag), *ko-bo* (kho bo); and ṅaṅ (ngang), ṅo (ngo), dṅo (dngo), dṅos (dngos), ṅogs (ngogs) may prob. be derived from the same root.

('I the first') pride, arrogance, frq.; ṅa-rgyál skyéd-pa (nga rgyal skyed pa) to be proud Dzl.; ɣcóg-pa (gcog pa) to break (another's pride), to humble, humiliate Mil.; ṅa-rgyál-càn (nga rgyal can) proud; W. also naughty, of children.
a species of duck, v. ṅúr-ba (ngur ba); perh. Anas casarca.
rarely for ṅán-pa (ngan pa); dri ṅá-ba (dri nga ba) stench Stg.; cf. nyám-ṅa-ba (nyam nga ba), yá-ṅa-ba (ya nga ba).

(cf. ṅad (ngad)) air, ṅa gáṅs-kyi ṅá-ras mi _jigs (nga gangs kyi nga ras mi 'jigs) I am not afraid of the air of glaciers Mil.; *ṅá-ra dáṅ-mo rag, _jáṁ-po rag* (nga ra dang mo rag, 'jam po rag) W. I perceive the air to be cold, to be mild; esp. cold air, ṅá-ra-càn (nga ra can) fresh, cold.

a loud voice, a cry, kye-húd-kyi ṅá-ro _bód-pa (kye hud kyi nga ro 'bod pa) to raise woeful cries Pth.; skád-kyi ṅá-ro čén-pos bsgrágs-so (skad kyi nga ro chen pos bsgrags so) they proclaimed, shouting at the top of their voices Pth.; séṅ-gei ṅá-ro (seng ge'i nga ro) the loud voice, the roaring, of a lion Mil.; ɣdúg-pai ṅá-ro (gdug pa'i nga ro) prob. voices foreboding mischief Mil.; the roar, roaring, rushing, of waves etc.; ṅá-ro sgróg-pa (nga ro sgrog pa) to roar, to rage; in a relative sense: skád-kyi ṅá-ro drag-žán (skad kyi nga ro drag zhan) a loud and a low sound, the different force or effort required in producing it Gram.; ṅá-ro-càn (nga ro can) loud, noisy, roaring; a crier, bawler, noisy fellow.

sometimes dṅags (dngags), resp. gsuṅ (gsung), speech, talk, word, ṅág-gi nyés-pa (ngag gi nyes pa) sins committed with the tongue, in words, (rdzun (rdzun), pŕá-ma (phra ma), tsig-rtsúb (tshig rtsub), prob. also kyál-ka (kyal ka)); ṅag-gi lhá-mo (ngag gi lha mo) the goddess of speech, of eloquence, Sarasvati; ṅág-gi dbaṅ-pýúg (ngag gi dbang phyug) = _jam-dbyáṅs ('jam dbyangs) Manjusri; ṅag _jáṁ-po (ngag 'jam po) kind, polite speech or words; ṅag-_jáṁ smra-mkás (ngag 'jam smra mkhas) of a soft tone in speaking and prudent in words Glr.; smán-pai ṅag bžín-du byed-pa (sman pa'i ngag bzhin du byed pa) to obey the words of the physician; ṅag sdóm-pa (ngag sdom pa), ṅag bcád-pa (ngag bcad pa) silence, as a monastic duty or religious exercise, resp. gsuṅ-bcád (gsung bcad) Mil.; gžan-gyi ṅag ɣcóg-pas (gzhan gyi ngag gcog pas) not doing according to another's word, not obeying him Tar., frq.; ṅag mnyán-pa (ngag mnyan pa) to be obedient Dzl. Comp. ṅag-kyál (ngag kyal), or -kyal (- kyal) = kyál-ka (kyal ka). — ṅag-grós (ngag gros), smrá-bai ṅag-grós (smra ba'i ngag gros) 'a manner of speaking or uttering words' Cs. — ṅag-rgyún (ngag rgyun) tradition, not recorded history, Cs. — ṅag-snyán (ngag snyan), snyan-ṅág (snyan ngag), snyan-dṅágs (snyan dngags) 1. poetical expression, figure, metaphor. 2. poem, piece of poetry Glr. — ṅag-dbáṅ (ngag dbang) 1. eloquent. 2. p. n., e. g. ṅag - dbáṅ blo - bzáṅ rgyá-mtso (ngag dbang blo bzang rgya mtsho) Dalai Lama, born 1615. — ṅag-sbyór (ngag sbyor) arrangement of speech Cs. — ṅag-tsíg (ngag tshig) = ṅag (ngag). — ṅag-lám žú-ba (ngag lam zhu ba) to apply to a person by word of mouth, resp.

(not in the earlier literature) 1. the nature, essentiality, idiocrasy of a person, the peculiarity of a thing, saṅs-rgyás-kyi ṅaṅ yin (sangs rgyas kyi ngang yin) he is (partaking) of the nature of Buddha, Buddha-like (corresponding to our 'divine', which consequently might be expressed by dkon-mčóg-gi ṅaṅ (dkon mchog gi ngang)) Mil.; stóṅ-pai ṅaṅ-nyíd (stong pa'i ngang nyid) the essentiality of the vacuum itself Glr.; frq. used only paraphrastically or pleonastically: tiṅ-ṅe-_dzín-gyi ṅaṅ-la žúgs-pa (ting nge 'dzin gyi ngang la zhugs pa) to enter into meditation Mil.; tugs-mnyés bžin-pai ṅaṅ-la (thugs mnyes bzhin pa'i ngang la) in a cheerful mood Mil.; čágs-med-kyi ṅaṅ-la gnás-par gyis (chags med kyi ngang la gnas par gyis) continue in that passionless state of mind Thgr.; _jigs-skrág-gi ṅaṅ-nas _či-ba ('jigs skrag gi ngang nas 'chi ba) to die of fear or anxiety; ṅaṅ-nas (ngang nas) in general is used nearly like sgó-nas (sgo nas) Mil. frq.; character, disposition, ṅaṅ-bzáṅ (ngang bzang), ṅaṅ-ṅán (ngang ngan) Sch.; ṅaṅ-tsúl (ngang tshul), and esp.
